topic.topic: When do multi-agent systems outperform single-agent systems?

topic_summary: First-principles research package for: When do multi-agent systems outperform single-agent systems?

primitive_definitions.name: Agent
primitive_definitions.definition: An autonomous entity that perceives its environment, makes decisions, and takes actions to achieve goals.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: It is the fundamental building block of both single-agent and multi-agent systems; cannot be defined without circularity.

primitive_definitions.name: Task
primitive_definitions.definition: A unit of work or objective that requires a set of actions to be accomplished.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: It defines what the system must achieve; without it, performance comparison is meaningless.

primitive_definitions.name: Performance
primitive_definitions.definition: A quantifiable measure of how well a system accomplishes its task(s), typically along dimensions such as speed, quality, cost, or robustness.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: It is the basis for comparison; any system can be evaluated on performance.

assumptions.assumption: All agents in a multi-agent system are cooperative and share a common goal.
assumptions.rationale: This simplifies analysis by removing adversarial interactions and focusing on coordination benefits.
assumptions.possible_failure: In real-world scenarios, agents may have conflicting goals, leading to different performance dynamics such as competition or negotiation.

assumptions.assumption: Tasks can be decomposed into subtasks that can be executed in parallel or sequentially by multiple agents.
assumptions.rationale: Many complex tasks are naturally decomposable, allowing for parallel execution and potential speedup.
assumptions.possible_failure: Some tasks are inherently sequential or require tight coupling, making decomposition inefficient or impossible, thus favoring a single agent.

variables.name: Task_Decomposability
variables.description: Degree to which the task can be split into independent subtasks.

variables.name: Number_of_Agents
variables.description: Number of agents in the multi-agent system.

variables.name: Communication_Cost
variables.description: Cost or overhead of communication between agents.

variables.name: Agent_Specialization
variables.description: Diversity of agent capabilities; higher specialization allows division of labor.

variables.name: Parallelism_Gain
variables.description: Speedup from executing subtasks in parallel.

variables.name: Synergy_Benefit
variables.description: Additional performance gain from agents specializing and covering more skills.

variables.name: Coordination_Overhead
variables.description: Time/resources spent on coordinating actions and sharing information.

variables.name: Relative_Performance
variables.description: Ratio of multi-agent system performance to single-agent system performance.

causal_chain.explanation: Tasks that can be decomposed allow parallel execution, leading to potential speedup.
More agents can execute more subtasks in parallel, but only up to the decomposition limit.
Higher communication cost raises overhead for coordination among agents.
Specialized agents can complement each other, improving overall capability.
Diverse agents require more coordination to integrate their contributions.
Parallel execution reduces total time, improving performance.
Synergy allows multi-agent system to handle more complex tasks.
Overhead consumes resources, reducing net gain.

bottlenecks.why_it_matters: If the task cannot be decomposed, parallelism gain is negligible, and overhead may dominate.
Excessive communication overhead can negate parallelism gains, especially with many agents.
Without complementary skills, multi-agent system may not outperform a single versatile agent.

mechanism_model.mechanism_summary: Multi-agent systems outperform single-agent systems when the task is highly decomposable, communication cost is low, and agents have diverse specialized skills that create synergy. The mechanism involves a trade-off between parallelism gains and synergy benefits versus coordination overhead. The key causal pathway is that decomposability and number of agents increase parallelism, while specialization increases both synergy and coordination cost. The net performance advantage is determined by whether the sum of parallelism and synergy exceeds coordination overhead.

tensions.statement: The assumption that all agents are fully cooperative may fail in real-world scenarios where agents have conflicting goals, altering the performance dynamics of multi-agent systems.
tensions.why_it_matters: If agents are competitive, coordination overhead increases and synergy decreases, potentially reversing the advantage of multi-agent systems.
tensions.what_existing_methods_may_miss: Existing benchmarks often assume pure cooperation, ignoring mixed-motive or adversarial interactions.

tensions.statement: There is a tradeoff between communication overhead and parallelism gain: increasing the number of agents boosts parallelism but also raises communication costs, leading to an optimal number of agents.
tensions.why_it_matters: Neglecting this tradeoff may lead to overestimating the benefits of adding more agents; existing methods often assume linear scaling.
tensions.what_existing_methods_may_miss: Linear models of speedup ignore the nonlinear impact of communication overhead.

tensions.statement: Specialized agents can create synergy but also incur higher coordination overhead; the net effect depends on task structure and agent diversity.
tensions.why_it_matters: If coordination overhead outweighs synergy, multi-agent systems may underperform single-agent systems despite specialization.
tensions.what_existing_methods_may_miss: Many multi-agent learning methods ignore the cost of coordination and assume synergy always dominates.

tensions.statement: The assumption that tasks are decomposable into independent subtasks is not always valid; for tightly coupled tasks, parallelism gain is negligible and multi-agent systems may be worse.
tensions.why_it_matters: This assumption underpins the potential speedup in many models; its failure means multi-agent systems are only beneficial for certain task types.
tensions.what_existing_methods_may_miss: Benchmarks often use decomposable tasks, leading to overgeneralization of multi-agent advantages.

candidate_questions.question: Does introducing mixed-motive incentives in a multi-agent system cause a decrease in relative performance compared to a fully cooperative setting, mediated by increased coordination overhead?
candidate_questions.expected_contribution: Reveals boundary conditions for the assumption of full cooperation, showing that competitive goals can negate multi-agent advantages.

candidate_questions.question: What is the functional relationship between the number of agents and relative performance under varying communication costs, and at what point does coordination overhead negate parallelism gains?
candidate_questions.expected_contribution: Identifies the optimal number of agents for given communication costs, challenging linear scaling assumptions.

candidate_questions.question: How does the relative performance of multi-agent systems with specialized agents vary with task structure and coordination overhead?
candidate_questions.expected_contribution: Characterizes the conditions under which specialization yields net benefits, highlighting when coordination costs dominate synergy.

candidate_questions.question: Does decreasing task decomposability causally reduce the relative performance advantage of multi-agent systems by limiting parallelism gain?
candidate_questions.expected_contribution: Establishes task decomposability as a necessary condition for multi-agent speedup, preventing overgeneralization from decomposable benchmarks.

certificates.topic: When do multi-agent systems outperform single-agent systems?

first_principle_assumptions.possible_failure: Some tasks are inherently sequential or require tight coupling, making decomposition inefficient or impossible, thus favoring a single agent.

More agents can execute more subtasks in parallel, but only up to the decomposition limit.
Higher communication cost raises overhead for coordination among agents.
Specialized agents can complement each other, improving overall capability.
Diverse agents require more coordination to integrate their contributions.
Parallel execution reduces total time, improving performance.
Synergy allows multi-agent system to handle more complex tasks.
Overhead consumes resources, reducing net gain.

bottlenecks.why_it_matters: If the task cannot be decomposed, parallelism gain is negligible, and overhead may dominate.
Excessive communication overhead can negate parallelism gains, especially with many agents.
Without complementary skills, multi-agent system may not outperform a single versatile agent.

mechanism_model.mechanism_summary: Multi-agent systems outperform single-agent systems when the task is highly decomposable, communication cost is low, and agents have diverse specialized skills that create synergy. The mechanism involves a trade-off between parallelism gains and synergy benefits versus coordination overhead. The key causal pathway is that decomposability and number of agents increase parallelism, while specialization increases both synergy and coordination cost. The net performance advantage is determined by whether the sum of parallelism and synergy exceeds coordination overhead.

tension_or_contradiction.statement: The assumption that all agents are fully cooperative may fail in real-world scenarios where agents have conflicting goals, altering the performance dynamics of multi-agent systems.
tension_or_contradiction.why_it_matters: If agents are competitive, coordination overhead increases and synergy decreases, potentially reversing the advantage of multi-agent systems.
tension_or_contradiction.what_existing_methods_may_miss: Existing benchmarks often assume pure cooperation, ignoring mixed-motive or adversarial interactions.

research_question.question: Is there a critical threshold of incentive conflict in multi-agent systems above which they underperform single-agent systems, and does this threshold depend on task decomposability?
research_question.expected_contribution: Reveals a phase transition in multi-agent advantage driven by incentive structure, moderated by task decomposability, providing a precise boundary condition for when multi-agent systems succeed or fail.

hypothesis.statement: The relative performance of multi-agent to single-agent systems exhibits a phase transition as incentive conflict increases: below a threshold conflict level, multi-agent systems outperform; above it, they underperform. The threshold conflict level increases with task decomposability.
hypothesis.predicted_effect: Relative performance (multi-agent vs single-agent) will be above 1 for low conflict and below 1 for high conflict, with a sharp drop at the threshold. Higher task decomposability shifts the threshold to higher conflict levels.

minimal_decisive_test.experiment: In a simulated multi-agent task (e.g., grid-world with resource collection or coordinated box pushing), vary both incentive conflict (via mixture of shared and individual rewards: 0% conflict = fully cooperative, 50% equal mixture, 100% fully individual) and task decomposability (low: tasks require tight coupling; high: tasks can be split into independent subtasks). Use a single-agent baseline with the same overall capability. Measure relative performance (ratio of success rate or completion time) and coordination overhead (e.g., messages). Analyze for a discontinuous drop in relative performance as conflict increases, and record the conflict level where the drop occurs for each decomposability level.
independent_variables: Incentive conflict level (continuous: 0 to 1, varying mixture of shared and individual rewards)
dependent_variables: Relative performance (ratio of multi-agent to single-agent metric)
controls: Agent capabilities (identical except for potential specialization)
Evaluation metric (e.g., completion time or success rate)
minimal_decisive_test.falsifying_observation: If relative performance does not show a sharp drop but instead declines linearly or remains above 1 across all conflict levels, or if the threshold does not vary with task decomposability, the hypothesis is falsified.

supports_hypothesis: Relative performance drops sharply at a specific conflict threshold, transitioning from above 1 to below 1.
The conflict threshold is higher for high task decomposability than for low.
Coordination overhead increases steeply near the threshold.
rejects_hypothesis: Relative performance declines linearly with conflict without a clear threshold.
Relative performance remains above 1 for all conflict levels (i.e., multi-agent always better).
Relative performance remains below 1 for all conflict levels (i.e., single-agent always better).
The threshold does not shift with decomposability.

failure_update_rule.if_failed: If no sharp threshold is found or the threshold does not depend on decomposability, then the assumed phase transition and moderating effect of decomposability are not supported. This suggests that the boundary condition for multi-agent advantage may be more complex, possibly involving other factors like agent specialization or communication cost.
failure_update_rule.assumption_to_revise: Assumption that task decomposability is the primary moderator of the incentive conflict threshold.
failure_update_rule.next_question: What other mechanism (e.g., agent specialization diversity, communication protocol) determines the critical level of incentive conflict where multi-agent systems fail?

primitive_definitions.why_primitive: It is the basis for comparison; any system can be evaluated on performance.

first_principle_assumptions.assumption: All agents in a multi-agent system are cooperative and share a common goal.
first_principle_assumptions.rationale: This simplifies analysis by removing adversarial interactions and focusing on coordination benefits.
first_principle_assumptions.possible_failure: In real-world scenarios, agents may have conflicting goals, leading to different performance dynamics such as competition or negotiation.

first_principle_assumptions.rationale: Many complex tasks are naturally decomposable, allowing for parallel execution and potential speedup.
first_principle_assumptions.possible_failure: Some tasks are inherently sequential or require tight coupling, making decomposition inefficient or impossible, thus favoring a single agent.

variables.description: Degree to which the task can be split into independent subtasks.

causal_chain.explanation: Tasks that can be decomposed allow parallel execution, leading to potential speedup.
More agents can execute more subtasks in parallel, but only up to the decomposition limit.
Higher communication cost raises overhead for coordination among agents.
Specialized agents can complement each other, improving overall capability.
Diverse agents require more coordination to integrate their contributions.
Parallel execution reduces total time, improving performance.
Synergy allows multi-agent system to handle more complex tasks.
Overhead consumes resources, reducing net gain.

bottlenecks.why_it_matters: If the task cannot be decomposed, parallelism gain is negligible, and overhead may dominate.
Excessive communication overhead can negate parallelism gains, especially with many agents.
Without complementary skills, multi-agent system may not outperform a single versatile agent.

mechanism_model.mechanism_summary: Multi-agent systems outperform single-agent systems when the task is highly decomposable, communication cost is low, and agents have diverse specialized skills that create synergy. The mechanism involves a trade-off between parallelism gains and synergy benefits versus coordination overhead. The key causal pathway is that decomposability and number of agents increase parallelism, while specialization increases both synergy and coordination cost. The net performance advantage is determined by whether the sum of parallelism and synergy exceeds coordination overhead.

tension_or_contradiction.statement: There is a tradeoff between communication overhead and parallelism gain: increasing the number of agents boosts parallelism but also raises communication costs, leading to an optimal number of agents.
tension_or_contradiction.why_it_matters: Neglecting this tradeoff may lead to overestimating the benefits of adding more agents; existing methods often assume linear scaling.
tension_or_contradiction.what_existing_methods_may_miss: Linear models of speedup ignore the nonlinear impact of communication overhead.

research_question.question: At what critical communication cost threshold does adding more agents transition from performance improvement to degradation relative to a single agent, and how does this threshold depend on task decomposability?
research_question.expected_contribution: Identifies a sharp phase transition boundary in the performance of multi-agent systems, challenging smooth scaling assumptions.

hypothesis.statement: Relative performance of multi-agent over single-agent systems undergoes a phase transition as a function of the ratio of communication cost to task decomposability: below a critical threshold, adding agents improves relative performance up to an optimum; above that threshold, adding agents monotonically degrades performance, and the optimal number of agents drops to zero (i.e., single-agent is best). The critical threshold is determined by the slope of coordination overhead scaling.
hypothesis.predicted_effect: There exists a critical communication cost value beyond which multi-agent systems never outperform a single agent, regardless of the number of agents; and the transition is sharp, not gradual.

minimal_decisive_test.experiment: Simulate a cooperative multi-agent system performing a decomposable task (e.g., parallel grid search) with constant task decomposability (fixed number of independent subtasks). Vary the number of agents from 1 to 64 in powers of two, and vary communication cost (low, medium, high, very high) by introducing a fixed delay per message. For each configuration, measure the total completion time and compute relative performance (single-agent time / multi-agent time). Perform 50 repetitions per configuration to obtain mean and variance. Then, for each communication cost level, plot relative performance vs. number of agents and identify whether there is a clear threshold where relative performance drops below 1 for all agent counts.
controls: Task_Decomposability (set to high, e.g., 64 independent subtasks)
minimal_decisive_test.falsifying_observation: If for all communication cost levels, relative performance exceeds 1 for some number of agents (i.e., multi-agent can always beat single-agent with appropriate agent count), then the hypothesis of a critical threshold is falsified. Alternatively, if relative performance decreases smoothly without a sharp transition, the phase transition claim is falsified.

supports_hypothesis: At low communication cost, relative performance peaks above 1 for intermediate number of agents then declines.
At a specific critical communication cost, relative performance reaches exactly 1 at the peak and never exceeds 1 for any agent count.
Above the critical cost, relative performance is always below 1, and the optimal agent count is 1 (single-agent beats multi-agent).
The transition from multi-agent advantage to disadvantage happens abruptly as communication cost crosses the critical threshold.
rejects_hypothesis: For all communication cost levels, there exists some number of agents where relative performance > 1 (no universal underperformance regime).
Relative performance shows only gradual decline with increasing communication cost, without a distinct critical point.
The predicted critical threshold does not exist; multi-agent systems can always be made to outperform with enough agents.

failure_update_rule.if_failed: If no critical threshold is observed and multi-agent always outperforms with enough agents, then the hypothesis that coordination overhead inevitably causes underperformance at high cost is wrong.
failure_update_rule.assumption_to_revise: The assumption that coordination overhead scales linearly with the number of agents may be wrong; it could saturate or be sublinear, allowing multi-agent to always exceed single-agent for large agent counts.
failure_update_rule.next_question: What is the scaling law of coordination overhead with number of agents in cooperative multi-agent systems, and how does it affect the asymptotic performance relative to a single agent?

primitive_definitions.definition: A quantifiable measure of how well a system accomplishes its task(s), typically along dimensions such as speed, quality, cost, or robustness.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: It is the basis for comparison; any system can be evaluated on performance.

first_principle_assumptions.rationale: This simplifies analysis by removing adversarial interactions and focusing on coordination benefits.
first_principle_assumptions.possible_failure: In real-world scenarios, agents may have conflicting goals, leading to different performance dynamics such as competition or negotiation.

causal_chain.explanation: Tasks that can be decomposed allow parallel execution, leading to potential speedup.
More agents can execute more subtasks in parallel, but only up to the decomposition limit.
Higher communication cost raises overhead for coordination among agents.
Specialized agents can complement each other, improving overall capability.
Diverse agents require more coordination to integrate their contributions.
Parallel execution reduces total time, improving performance.
Synergy allows multi-agent system to handle more complex tasks.
Overhead consumes resources, reducing net gain.

bottlenecks.why_it_matters: If the task cannot be decomposed, parallelism gain is negligible, and overhead may dominate.
Excessive communication overhead can negate parallelism gains, especially with many agents.
Without complementary skills, multi-agent system may not outperform a single versatile agent.

mechanism_model.mechanism_summary: Multi-agent systems outperform single-agent systems when the task is highly decomposable, communication cost is low, and agents have diverse specialized skills that create synergy. The mechanism involves a trade-off between parallelism gains and synergy benefits versus coordination overhead. The key causal pathway is that decomposability and number of agents increase parallelism, while specialization increases both synergy and coordination cost. The net performance advantage is determined by whether the sum of parallelism and synergy exceeds coordination overhead.

tension_or_contradiction.statement: Specialized agents can create synergy but also incur higher coordination overhead; the net effect depends on task structure and agent diversity.
tension_or_contradiction.why_it_matters: If coordination overhead outweighs synergy, multi-agent systems may underperform single-agent systems despite specialization.
tension_or_contradiction.what_existing_methods_may_miss: Many multi-agent learning methods ignore the cost of coordination and assume synergy always dominates.

research_question.question: What is the critical ratio of communication cost to task decomposability that determines when multi-agent systems outperform single-agent systems, and how does agent specialization shift this threshold?
research_question.expected_contribution: Identifies a precise phase transition boundary between regimes where multi-agent systems are advantageous versus disadvantageous, and quantifies how specialization modulates that boundary.

hypothesis.statement: There exists a threshold θ such that when Communication_Cost / Task_Decomposability < θ, multi-agent systems with specialized agents achieve higher performance than a single agent; when the ratio exceeds θ, single-agent systems are superior. Furthermore, θ increases with Agent_Specialization, meaning specialization expands the regime where multi-agent systems are beneficial.
hypothesis.predicted_effect: Relative performance (multi-agent vs single-agent) exhibits a sharp discontinuity at the threshold ratio, and the location of this discontinuity shifts rightward as specialization increases.

minimal_decisive_test.experiment: Simulate a task with variable decomposability (D, number of independent subtasks, range 1 to 20) and communication cost (C, latency per message, range 0 to 10). Use 5 agents with either homogeneous or heterogeneous specialized skills. For each combination (D, C), measure completion time ratio R = T_multi / T_single. Perform binary search or grid search to find the contour where R=1. Compare the threshold ratio C/D between specialization levels. Replicate across multiple random seeds.
dependent_variables: Relative Performance (R = multi-agent completion time / single-agent completion time)
minimal_decisive_test.falsifying_observation: If no clear threshold exists (R varies smoothly without crossing 1), or if the threshold does not depend on specialization, the hypothesis is falsified.

supports_hypothesis: For each specialization level, there is a sharp boundary in the (C, D) plane where R changes from <1 to >1.
The boundary is well-described by a constant ratio C/D for a given specialization.
Heterogeneous specialization shifts the boundary to higher C/D values (i.e., larger region where multi-agent is better).
rejects_hypothesis: R remains above 1 for all (C, D) tested, indicating multi-agent never outperforms.
R is always below 1, indicating multi-agent always outperforms.
The transition is gradual and no consistent ratio threshold can be identified.
The threshold does not change with specialization.

failure_update_rule.if_failed: If no threshold is observed or threshold is independent of specialization,
failure_update_rule.assumption_to_revise: The assumption that specialization affects the trade-off linearly; perhaps coordination overhead grows nonlinearly with number of specializations and dominates even at low communication cost.
failure_update_rule.next_question: How does the shape of the coordination overhead function (linear vs superlinear) determine the existence of a phase transition?

mechanism_model.mechanism_summary: Multi-agent systems outperform single-agent systems when the task is highly decomposable, communication cost is low, and agents have diverse specialized skills that create synergy. The mechanism involves a trade-off between parallelism gains and synergy benefits versus coordination overhead. The key causal pathway is that decomposability and number of agents increase parallelism, while specialization increases both synergy and coordination cost. The net performance advantage is determined by whether the sum of parallelism and synergy exceeds coordination overhead.

tension_or_contradiction.statement: The assumption that tasks are decomposable into independent subtasks is not always valid; for tightly coupled tasks, parallelism gain is negligible and multi-agent systems may be worse.
tension_or_contradiction.why_it_matters: This assumption underpins the potential speedup in many models; its failure means multi-agent systems are only beneficial for certain task types.
tension_or_contradiction.what_existing_methods_may_miss: Benchmarks often use decomposable tasks, leading to overgeneralization of multi-agent advantages.

research_question.question: Is there a critical threshold of task decomposability below which multi-agent systems become inferior to single-agent systems, and if so, what is the functional form of the performance drop?
research_question.expected_contribution: Identifies a sharp phase transition in the advantage of multi-agent systems, moving from a linear relationship to a threshold-based understanding, preventing overgeneralization from high-decomposability benchmarks.

hypothesis.statement: There exists a decomposability threshold D_c such that for decomposability > D_c, multi-agent systems outperform single-agent systems (relative performance ratio < 1), and for decomposability < D_c, they underperform (ratio > 1). The transition is sigmoidal around D_c.
hypothesis.predicted_effect: The relative performance ratio will show a sharp increase (worsening of multi-agent) as decomposability crosses below D_c, rather than a gradual linear decline.

minimal_decisive_test.experiment: Use a simulated grid-world task where agents collect items. Decomposability is varied continuously by controlling the spatial dependency of items: from fully independent (parallel) to fully sequential (serial). Use at least 5 levels of decomposability (e.g., 0.9, 0.7, 0.5, 0.3, 0.1). Multi-agent system with N=5 agents vs single-agent system. Communication cost is fixed at a low constant. All agents identical. Measure completion time for each decomposability level, compute relative performance ratio. Fit a sigmoid to estimate D_c.
dependent_variables: Relative performance ratio (multi-agent time / single-agent time)
minimal_decisive_test.falsifying_observation: If the relative performance ratio does not exhibit a clear threshold (e.g., it decreases monotonically with decomposability, or the ratio is always below 1, or the transition is linear without a plateau), the hypothesis is falsified.

supports_hypothesis: At high decomposability (0.9), multi-agent system significantly outperforms single-agent (ratio < 0.8).
At intermediate levels, there is a distinct drop in performance advantage, with ratio crossing 1.0 near D_c.
At low decomposability (0.1), multi-agent system is significantly worse (ratio > 1.2).
The relationship follows a sigmoidal curve with a steep transition around D_c.
rejects_hypothesis: Relative performance ratio remains constant across decomposability levels.
Relative performance ratio decreases monotonically (multi-agent advantage increases) as decomposability decreases.
Multi-agent system outperforms single-agent at all decomposability levels.
The transition is linear without a clear threshold.

failure_update_rule.if_failed: If no threshold is found (e.g., ratio stays below 1 for all levels), then decomposability alone is not the controlling factor; the advantage may be due to other mechanisms like synergy.
failure_update_rule.assumption_to_revise: The assumption that task decomposability is a necessary condition for speedup is too narrow; other factors (e.g., agent specialization, communication cost) may create a different boundary.
failure_update_rule.next_question: Under what conditions can multi-agent systems outperform single-agent systems even when tasks have low decomposability? Does specialization compensate for lack of parallelism?

experiment_plans.hypothesis: The relative performance of multi-agent to single-agent systems exhibits a phase transition as incentive conflict increases: below a threshold conflict level, multi-agent systems outperform; above it, they underperform. The threshold conflict level increases with task decomposability.
independent_variables: Incentive conflict level (0 to 1, mixture of shared and individual rewards)
dependent_variables: Relative performance (ratio of multi-agent to single-agent metric)
controlled_variables: Agent capabilities (identical except for potential specialization)
Evaluation metric (e.g., completion time or success rate)

baselines.name: Single-agent system
baselines.description: A single agent with the same overall capability as the multi-agent system (sum of capabilities).

metrics.name: Relative performance
metrics.definition: Ratio of multi-agent system completion time (or success rate) to single-agent system completion time (or success rate). Values above 1 indicate multi-agent outperforms.

metrics.name: Coordination overhead
metrics.definition: Average number of messages exchanged between agents per timestep.

experiment_plans.expected_result: Relative performance drops sharply from above 1 to below 1 at a specific conflict threshold; the threshold is higher for high task decomposability than for low; coordination overhead increases steeply near the threshold.
experiment_plans.falsifying_result: Relative performance declines linearly with conflict without a sharp drop, or remains above/below 1 across all conflict levels, or threshold does not vary with decomposability.
experiment_plans.minimum_viable_experiment: Simulate a grid-world task (e.g., resource collection or box pushing) with 3 agents. Vary incentive conflict (0%, 50%, 100% mixture) and task decomposability (low: tasks require tight coupling; high: tasks can be split into independent subtasks). Measure relative performance and coordination overhead. Use a single agent with equivalent total capability as baseline. Analyze for discontinuous drop in relative performance.
risks_and_confounds: Number of agents fixed at 3 may not generalize to other agent counts
Agent specialization not explicitly controlled; may confound results
Communication bandwidth fixed; higher bandwidth might reduce overhead
Specific task design (e.g., grid-world) may bias results

experiment_plans.hypothesis: Relative performance of multi-agent over single-agent systems undergoes a phase transition as a function of the ratio of communication cost to task decomposability: below a critical threshold, adding agents improves relative performance up to an optimum; above that threshold, adding agents monotonically degrades performance, and the optimal number of agents drops to zero (i.e., single-agent is best). The critical threshold is determined by the slope of coordination overhead scaling.

baselines.name: Single-Agent Performance
baselines.description: Performance of a single agent completing the entire task alone, used as the denominator in relative performance.

metrics.name: Completion Time
metrics.definition: Total time taken for the system to complete the task.

metrics.name: Relative Performance
metrics.definition: Single-agent completion time divided by multi-agent completion time.

experiment_plans.expected_result: At low communication cost, relative performance peaks above 1 for intermediate agent counts. At a critical communication cost, relative performance reaches exactly 1 at the peak and never exceeds 1. Above that cost, relative performance is always below 1, indicating multi-agent never outperforms single-agent.
experiment_plans.falsifying_result: If for all communication cost levels tested, there exists some number of agents where relative performance > 1 (i.e., multi-agent can beat single-agent), or if relative performance declines smoothly without a sharp transition, then the hypothesis is falsified.
experiment_plans.minimum_viable_experiment: Simulate a cooperative multi-agent system performing a decomposable task (e.g., parallel grid search) with fixed task decomposability (64 independent subtasks). Vary the number of agents from 1 to 64 in powers of two (1,2,4,8,16,32,64). Vary communication cost at four levels: very low (negligible delay), low, medium, high, causing increasing overhead. For each configuration, measure total completion time and compute relative performance = single-agent time / multi-agent time. Run 50 repetitions per configuration to estimate mean and variance. Plot relative performance vs. number of agents for each communication cost level and identify if there is a clear threshold where relative performance drops below 1 for all agent counts.
risks_and_confounds: Communication cost model might not capture real-world overheads
Fixed task decomposability may limit generalizability
Agent specialization held constant; results may differ with specialized agents
Number of agents upper bound may be too low to observe asymptotic behavior

experiment_plans.hypothesis: There exists a threshold θ such that when Communication_Cost / Task_Decomposability < θ, multi-agent systems with specialized agents achieve higher performance than a single agent; when the ratio exceeds θ, single-agent systems are superior. Furthermore, θ increases with Agent_Specialization, meaning specialization expands the regime where multi-agent systems are beneficial.
dependent_variables: Relative Performance (R = multi-agent completion time / single-agent completion time)

baselines.name: Single agent
baselines.description: A single agent performs the entire task without decomposition.

baselines.name: Multi-agent with homogeneous agents
baselines.description: Multiple agents with identical capabilities (no specialization).

metrics.name: Multi-agent completion time
metrics.definition: Total time taken by the multi-agent system to complete the task.

metrics.name: Single-agent completion time
metrics.definition: Total time taken by a single agent to complete the task.

metrics.name: Relative performance R
metrics.definition: Ratio of multi-agent completion time to single-agent completion time.

metrics.name: Threshold ratio θ
metrics.definition: Value of Communication_Cost / Task_Decomposability at which R equals 1, estimated via interpolation.

experiment_plans.expected_result: For each specialization level, there is a sharp boundary in the (C, D) plane where R changes from <1 (multi-agent faster) to >1 (single-agent faster). The boundary is well-described by a constant ratio C/D for a given specialization. Heterogeneous specialization shifts the boundary to higher C/D values (i.e., larger region where multi-agent is better).
experiment_plans.falsifying_result: No clear threshold exists (R varies smoothly without crossing 1, or R always >1 or always <1) OR the threshold does not depend on specialization (i.e., the boundary is identical for homogeneous and heterogeneous agents).
experiment_plans.minimum_viable_experiment: Simulate a task with variable decomposability D (1 to 20 independent subtasks) and communication cost C (0 to 10 latency per message). Use 5 agents that are either homogeneous (all identical) or heterogeneous (each specialized in a subset of skills). For each (D, C, spec) combination, measure completion time ratio R. Perform a grid search to find the contour where R=1. Determine the ratio C/D at that contour for each specialization level. Replicate across multiple random seeds.
risks_and_confounds: Randomness in agent behavior could blur threshold; mitigated by deterministic execution.
Choice of specific task could confound; controlled by generic task with adjustable decomposability.
Abstraction of communication cost may not capture real overhead; but it is a controlled parameter.

experiment_plans.hypothesis: There exists a decomposability threshold D_c such that for decomposability > D_c, multi-agent systems outperform single-agent systems (relative performance ratio < 1), and for decomposability < D_c, they underperform (ratio > 1). The transition is sigmoidal around D_c.
dependent_variables: Relative performance ratio (multi-agent time / single-agent time)

baselines.name: Single-agent system
baselines.description: A single agent completes the same task alone under identical conditions.

metrics.name: Completion time
metrics.definition: Time taken to collect all items in the grid-world.

metrics.name: Relative performance ratio
metrics.definition: Multi-agent completion time divided by single-agent completion time.

experiment_plans.expected_result: At high decomposability (0.9), multi-agent system significantly outperforms single-agent (ratio < 0.8). At intermediate levels, there is a distinct drop in performance advantage, with ratio crossing 1.0 near D_c. At low decomposability (0.1), multi-agent system is significantly worse (ratio > 1.2). The relationship follows a sigmoidal curve with a steep transition around D_c.
experiment_plans.falsifying_result: Relative performance ratio remains constant across decomposability levels, decreases monotonically as decomposability decreases, multi-agent system outperforms single-agent at all levels, or the transition is linear without a clear threshold.
experiment_plans.minimum_viable_experiment: Use a simulated grid-world task where agents collect items. Decomposability is varied continuously by controlling the spatial dependency of items: from fully independent (parallel) to fully sequential (serial). Use at least 5 levels of decomposability (e.g., 0.9, 0.7, 0.5, 0.3, 0.1). Multi-agent system with N=5 agents vs single-agent system. Communication cost is fixed at a low constant. All agents identical. Measure completion time for each decomposability level, compute relative performance ratio. Fit a sigmoid to estimate D_c.
risks_and_confounds: Low number of decomposability levels might miss the threshold
Identical agents limit synergy and may not capture real-world specialization
Fixed communication cost may not reflect variable overhead
Task type (grid-world) may not generalize to other domains
